    },
    {
      "path": "src/core",
      "line_range": "1-100",
      "role": "Core module containing forge abstraction and pattern matching logic"
    },
    {
      "path": "src/core/forge.rs",
      "line_range": "1-200",
      "role": "Forge trait definition and GitHub implementation"
    },
    {
      "path": "src/core/scanner.rs",
      "line_range": "1-150",
      "role": "SecurityScanner implementation with forge integration"
    },
    {
      "path": "tests/",
      "line_range": "1-50",
      "role": "Integration and unit test suite"
    },
    {
      "path": "CONTRIBUTING.md",
      "line_range": "1-100",
      "role": "Contribution guidelines and development conventions"
    }
  ],
  "conventions": {
    "commits": "Conventional commits (feat:, fix:, docs:, test:, refactor:, chore:) with optional scope; likely DCO-signed based on enterprise context",
    "testing": "Unit tests colocated with modules using #[cfg(test)]; integration tests in tests/ directory; test modules follow pattern of testing public APIs and error cases",
    "linting": "Likely uses clippy with strict warnings; rustfmt for formatting; enforced via CI (GitHub Actions)",
    "error_handling": "Result<T, Error> pattern with custom error types; likely uses thiserror or anyhow; propagation via ? operator; context-rich error messages",
    "imports": "Organized by standard library, external crates, then internal modules; use statements grouped by category"
  },
  "patterns": [
    "Trait-based abstraction for forge providers (Forge trait with GitHub impl)",
    "Repository and credentials abstraction for multi-provider support",
    "Pattern matching engine with configurable rules and secret detection",
    "Error handling via Result types with context propagation",
    "Configuration-driven behavior with serde for serialization",
    "Async runtime integration (likely tokio-based)"
  ],
  "related_issues": [
    {
      "number": 735,
      "title": "Multi-forge support architecture discussion",
      "relevance": "Foundational issue establishing requirements for GitLab, Gitea/Forgejo, and Azure DevOps support"
    },
    {
      "number": 1205,
      "title": "feat(core): multi-forge support for GitLab, Gitea/Forgejo, and Azure DevOps",
      "relevance": "Target issue requesting implementation of multi-forge support"
    }
  ],
  "constraints": [
    "Must maintain backward compatibility with existing GitHub integration",
    "API rate limiting and authentication handling varies significantly across forges",
    "Repository metadata schema differs between platforms (e.g., visibility, branch protection)",
    "Webhook/event systems have different payload structures and authentication mechanisms",
    "Credential storage and rotation must support multiple authentication schemes (OAuth, PAT, SSH keys)",
    "Pattern matching rules must be forge-agnostic while accommodating platform-specific secret formats"
  ],
  "test_coverage": "Existing test coverage likely includes unit tests for GitHub forge implementation, pattern matching engine, and scanner core logic. Integration tests probably cover end-to-end scanning workflows. Multi-forge support will require expanding test suite to cover GitLab, Gitea/Forgejo, and Azure DevOps implementations with mocked API responses and fixture data.",
  "library_findings": [
    {
      "library": "reqwest or hyper",
      "version": "latest stable",
      "relevant_api": "HTTP client for forge API interactions; async/await support; connection pooling; retry logic",
      "notes": "Used for making authenticated requests to forge APIs; must support multiple authentication schemes (Bearer tokens, Basic auth, custom headers)"
    },
    {
      "library": "serde and serde_json",
      "version": "latest stable",
      "relevant_api": "Serialization/deserialization for API responses and configuration; derive macros for struct mapping",
      "notes": "Essential for parsing heterogeneous API response schemas from different forges; enables flexible configuration management"
    },
    {
      "library": "tokio",
      "version": "latest stable",
      "relevant_api": "Async runtime; task spawning; channel communication",
      "notes": "Likely used for concurrent API requests and background scanning tasks; enables efficient multi-forge operations"
    }
  ],
  "approaches": [
    {
      "name": "Trait-based Provider Abstraction with Adapter Pattern",
      "description": "Extend existing Forge trait to define common interface for repository discovery, secret scanning, and metadata retrieval. Implement concrete adapters for GitLab, Gitea/Forgejo, and Azure DevOps. Each adapter handles platform-specific API calls, authentication, and response mapping. Create a ForgeFactory or registry pattern to instantiate providers based on configuration. Maintain a shared pattern matching engine that operates on normalized repository and secret data structures.",
      "pros": [
        "Leverages existing Forge trait architecture; minimal disruption to current code",
        "Clear separation of concerns; each provider implementation isolated",
        "Easy to add new forges in future without modifying core scanning logic",
        "Testable in isolation; mock implementations straightforward",
        "Supports runtime forge selection and multi-forge scanning in single invocation"
      ],
      "cons": [
        "Requires careful API normalization to handle platform differences (e.g., visibility enums, branch naming)",
        "Authentication complexity multiplied; must handle OAuth, PAT, SSH keys per platform",
        "Rate limiting strategies differ; may need provider-specific throttling logic",
        "Webhook integration becomes complex if event-driven scanning is required",
        "Testing matrix expands significantly (3+ providers × multiple auth methods)"
      ],
      "complexity": "medium",
      "files_touched": 12
    },
    {
      "name": "Unified API Gateway with Normalization Layer",
      "description": "Create an intermediate abstraction layer that translates forge-specific API responses into canonical data models. Implement a ForgeClient trait that defines operations (list_repos, get_secrets, etc.) and a set of normalized types (NormalizedRepo, NormalizedSecret, NormalizedUser). Each forge adapter implements ForgeClient and handles translation from platform-specific schemas. The scanner operates exclusively on normalized types, decoupling business logic from forge specifics. Use serde with custom deserializers to handle schema mapping.",
      "pros": [
        "Maximum decoupling between forge implementations and core scanning logic",
        "Easier to reason about and test core functionality independent of forge details",
        "Normalized types enable consistent error handling and logging across forges",
        "Facilitates future addition of forge-agnostic features (e.g., cross-forge reporting)",
        "Reduces cognitive load when adding new forges; clear contract to implement"
      ],
      "cons": [
        "Additional abstraction layer introduces performance overhead (object mapping, translation)",
        "Risk of losing platform-specific information during normalization; may require metadata pass-through",
        "Requires upfront investment in designing comprehensive normalized schema",
        "Debugging becomes harder; must trace through translation layers",
        "May need versioning strategy for normalized types if schema evolves"
      ],
      "complexity": "complex",
      "files_touched": 15
    },
    {
      "name": "Configuration-Driven Multi-Forge Registry with Pluggable Handlers",
      "description": "Implement a forge registry system where each forge is registered with a handler struct containing API endpoints, authentication logic, and response parsers. Use a configuration file (YAML/TOML) to specify active forges and their credentials. Handlers are registered at startup and selected based on repository URL scheme or explicit configuration. Leverage trait objects and dynamic dispatch to invoke handler methods. Pattern matching engine remains forge-agnostic; handlers normalize data before passing to scanner.",
      "pros": [
        "Configuration-driven approach enables runtime forge selection without recompilation",
        "Reduces code duplication; common patterns extracted into handler utilities",
        "Supports gradual migration; can enable forges incrementally",
        "Easier for operators to manage multiple forge instances",
        "Extensible without modifying core code; new handlers added via plugins or configuration"
      ],
      "cons": [
        "Configuration complexity; operators must understand all forge-specific settings",
        "Error messages less helpful if configuration is incorrect; harder to debug",
        "Handler registration and initialization adds startup overhead",
        "Trait objects and dynamic dispatch introduce runtime performance cost",
        "Testing requires comprehensive configuration fixtures"
      ],
      "complexity": "medium",
      "files_touched": 10
    }
  ],
  "recommendation": "Approach 1 (Trait-based Provider Abstraction with Adapter Pattern) is recommended. It balances pragmatism with extensibility, leveraging the existing Forge trait architecture that the codebase already uses. This approach minimizes disruption to current code while providing a clear path to add GitLab, Gitea/Forgejo, and Azure DevOps support. The adapter pattern is well-understood in Rust ecosystems and makes testing straightforward. Approach 2 offers superior long-term maintainability but requires significant upfront investment in schema design and normalization logic. Approach 3 introduces unnecessary complexity for the stated requirements; configuration-driven dispatch is overkill unless the project anticipates frequent forge additions or operator-driven extensibility. Start with Approach 1, implement adapters for the three requested forges, and refactor toward Approach 2 if the codebase grows to 5+ forge providers."
}
